data.title: Beacon Lighting Can Shine as Inflection Point Looms — Market Talk
data.description: Beacon Lighting is nearing an inflection point in the rate of earnings growth, says Morgans, as momentum in its trade business continues and lower interest rates give retail sales a shot in the arm. Analyst Emily Porter says Beacon Lighting has around 5% of a A$2 billion trade market for lighting and ceiling fans in Australia. "Building up share from this low base can more than offset the effect of a subdued overall market," says Morgans, which has an add call on the stock. The bank also points to signs that the retail cycle is beginning to turn. "We expect this to gather force as the recent interest rate cut by the Reserve Bank of Australia stimulates a recovery in consumer confidence," Morgans says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Millennium & Copthorne Hotels Says No Final Dividend for Fiscal 2024 as Part of Takeover Bid Conditions
data.description:
Millennium & Copthorne Hotels New Zealand will not pay a final dividend for fiscal 2024 as part of the conditions under CDL Hotels Holdings New Zealand's takeover bid for the company, according to a Friday filing with the New Zealand bourse.

A request was lodged to waive the condition and allow Millennium & Copthorne to declare a dividend, but CDL Hotels rejected it.

data.title: Sluggish demand, high supplies pin Vietnam rates to over 3-year low
data.description:
Key points:
  • Vietnam rates at $380-390 per ton
  • Winter-spring harvest expected to peak next month - Vietnamese trader
  • Bangladesh to import 100,000 tons of white rice from Vietnam

Vietnam's rice export prices retreated to their lowest in more than three years amid sluggish demand and abundant supply, while Indian rates held steady near a 19-month trough.

"Prices fell on strong supplies while demand remains weak. The winter-spring harvest has started and is expected to peak next month," a trader based in the Mekong Delta province of An Giang said.

Vietnam's 5% broken rice (RI-VNBKN5-P1) was offered at $380-$390 per metric ton, lowest level since late August 2021, down from $397 last week, according to traders.

"Buyers from the Philippines have slowed down their purchases, but are gradually returning to buy as they think Vietnamese prices have bottomed," he added. The Philippines is Vietnam's largest rice export market.

Top exporter India's 5% broken parboiled variety (RI-INBKN5-P1) was quoted at $416 to $425 per tonne, unchanged from last week. The price levels were the lowest since late June 2023.

Indian 5% broken white rice was priced at $395 to $405 per tonne this week.

A Kolkata-based exporter attributed the reduced demand for Indian rice to the competitive prices of Vietnam's new season crop.

The Indian rupee hit a fresh record low earlier in February, which effectively increased traders' margins from overseas sales.

Thailand's 5% broken rice (RI-THBKN5-P1) hovered at $420 per tonne from a range of $415 to $420 last week.

Prices held due to exchange rate conditions but were under pressure from new supply from Vietnam and especially Indian exports, a trader said.

Demand from regular customers like Indonesia and the Philippines has also been quiet, said another trader.

Elsewhere, Bangladesh will import 100,000 tons of white rice from Vietnam under a state-to-state deal at $474.25 per ton, officials said, as it looks to curb rising prices that have been burdening consumers.

data.title: ROSEN, GLOBAL INVESTOR COUNSEL, Encourages Novo Nordisk A/S Investors to Secure Counsel Before Important Deadline in Securities Class Action – NVO
data.description:
NEW YORK, Feb. 20, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) —

WHY: Rosen Law Firm, a global investor rights law firm, reminds purchasers of securities of Novo Nordisk A/S between November 2, 2022 and December 19, 2024, both dates inclusive (the “Class Period”), of the important March 25, 2025 lead plaintiff deadline.

SO WHAT: If you purchased Novo Nordisk securities during the Class Period you may be entitled to compensation without payment of any out of pocket fees or costs through a contingency fee arrangement.

WHAT TO DO NEXT: To join the Novo Nordisk class action, go to https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=34168 or call Phillip Kim, Esq. at 866-767-3653 or email case@rosenlegal.com for more information. A class action lawsuit has already been filed. If you wish to serve as lead plaintiff, you must move the Court no later than March 25, 2025. A lead plaintiff is a representative party acting on behalf of other class members in directing the litigation.

WHY ROSEN LAW: We encourage investors to select qualified counsel with a track record of success in leadership roles. Often, firms issuing notices do not have comparable experience, resources, or any meaningful peer recognition. Many of these firms do not actually litigate securities class actions, but are merely middlemen that refer clients or partner with law firms that actually litigate the cases. Be wise in selecting counsel. The Rosen Law Firm represents investors throughout the globe, concentrating its practice in securities class actions and shareholder derivative litigation. Rosen Law Firm achieved the largest ever securities class action settlement against a Chinese Company at the time. Rosen Law Firm was Ranked No. 1 by ISS Securities Class Action Services for number of securities class action settlements in 2017. The firm has been ranked in the top 4 each year since 2013 and has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors. In 2019 alone the firm secured over $438 million for investors. In 2020, founding partner Laurence Rosen was named by law360 as a Titan of Plaintiffs’ Bar. Many of the firm’s attorneys have been recognized by Lawdragon and Super Lawyers.

DETAILS OF THE CASE: According to the lawsuit, during the Class Period, defendants provided overwhelmingly positive statements to investors concerning the details of, and expectations for, Novo Nordisk’s phase 3 CagriSema study on obesity, named “REDEFINE-1.” Defendants’ statements failed to disclose or otherwise misled investors as to the nature of the dosages provided to patients in the study. Defendants’ statements further included, among other things, significant confidence in Novo Nordisk’s expectations for the study, in particular a minimum expected 25% average weight loss for obesity patients treated with CagriSema in the REDEFINE-1 study. Defendants provided these overwhelmingly positive statements to investors while, at the same time, disseminating materially false and misleading statements and/or concealing material adverse facts concerning the true state of Novo Nordisk’s REDEFINE-1 trial protocol; notably, that it was a “flexible protocol” which gave patients the ability “to modify their dosing throughout the trial.” When the true details entered the market, the lawsuit claims that investors suffered damages.

To join the Novo Nordisk class action, go to https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=34168 or call Phillip Kim, Esq. at 866-767-3653 or email case@rosenlegal.com for more information.

No Class Has Been Certified. Until a class is certified, you are not represented by counsel unless you retain one. You may select counsel of your choice. You may also remain an absent class member and do nothing at this point. An investor’s ability to share in any potential future recovery is not dependent upon serving as lead plaintiff.

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-rosen-law-firm, on Twitter: https://twitter.com/rosen_firm or on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/rosenlawfirm/.

Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee a similar outcome.

data.title: Australian court rules Telstra misled customers about internet speed
data.description:
Australia's federal court has ruled that telecom firm Telstra misled customers about the downgrading of its broadband plan's upload speed, the country's competition regulator said on Friday.

The Australian Competition & Consumer Commission (ACCC) had initiated the lawsuit against the company in December 2022, alleging Telstra downgraded broadband upload speed for nearly 9,000 residential customers in October and November 2020 without informing them or lowering its charges.

"Telstra's failure to inform customers that their broadband service had been altered denied them the opportunity to decide whether the changed service was suitable for their needs," ACCC Commissioner Liza Carver said in a statement.

The ACCC is seeking penalties, consumer compensation and other orders, with the final decisions to be made by the court at a later, yet-to-determined date.

"We expect better from the country's largest retail broadband internet service provider and believe these customers, who ultimately received a service they did not agree to, should be compensated," ACCC's Carver said.

Telstra did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Morocco Inflation Rate at 12-Month High of 2%
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Morocco increased to 2.0% in January 2025 from 0.7% in the previous month.

It marked the highest inflation rate since January 2024, with food and non-alcoholic beverage prices rising the most in a year (3.4% vs 0.6% in December 2024).

Also, inflation accelerated for alcoholic beverages and tobacco (3.0% vs 2.4%), while it remained stable for housing and utilities (at 3.6%), furniture, household items, and routine household maintenance (at 0.3%), and education (at 2.3%).

Meanwhile, inflation moderated for clothing and footwear (0.9% vs 1.3%), miscellaneous goods and services (2.0% vs 2.2%), recreation and culture (0.2% vs 0.3%), and restaurants and hotels (3.5% vs 3.8%), while communication prices were flat after edging up 0.1% in December.

Additionally, deflation eased for transport (-2.7% vs. -3.2%).

Monthly, consumer prices rose 0.8% in January, the first increase in five months, rebounding from a 0.2% fall in the previous month.

data.title: Floor & Decor Holdings Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Floor & Decor Holdings Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 37 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 34 cents. The mean expectation of twenty four analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 26 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 21 cents to 29 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 5.7% to $1.11 billion from a year ago; analysts expected $1.09 billion.

  • Floor & Decor Holdings Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 44 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $47.48 million.

  • Floor & Decor Holdings Inc shares had fallen by 4.4% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 3% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, two analysts negatively revised earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 7 "strong buy" or "buy," 16 "hold" and 3 "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The average consensus recommendation for the home improvement products & services retailers peer group is also "hold"

Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Floor & Decor Holdings Inc is $102.50

data.title: Japan Flash PMI Adds to Rate Hike Case — Market Talk
data.description: Japan's flash PMI data for February points to more rate hikes ahead, says Capital Economics' Marcel Thieliant. The composite PMI rose for a fourth month, consistent with annual GDP growth of 1%, above the economy's trend growth rate, the head of APAC says. The manufacturing PMI output component is consistent with falling industrial production, but new export orders point to rising export volumes, he notes. Output prices were little changed, but the index remains consistent with goods prices--ex-fresh food and energy--rising around 3% a year, in line with above-target inflation if services inflation stays around current levels. Coupled with the persistent strength in manufacturing output prices, the composite PMI suggests the Bank of Japan won't wait long before hiking again, he adds. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Hang Seng Tech Index Opens 3.4% Higher at 5684.94
Hang Seng Index Opens 2.3% Higher at 23090.03
Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2024 ET - Japan's flash PMI data for February points to more rate hikes ahead, says Capital Economics' Marcel Thieliant. The composite PMI rose for a fourth month, consistent with annual GDP growth of 1%, above the economy's trend growth rate, the head of APAC says. The manufacturing PMI output component is consistent with falling industrial production, but new export orders point to rising export volumes, he notes. Output prices were little changed, but the index remains consistent with goods prices--ex-fresh food and energy--rising around 3% a year, in line with above-target inflation if services inflation stays around current levels. Coupled with the persistent strength in manufacturing output prices, the composite PMI suggests the Bank of Japan won't wait long before hiking again, he adds. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

1952 ET - The yen weakens slightly against most other G-10 and Asian currencies in a likely technical correction following its recent rally. However, dollar-yen's "new year" downtrend appears to be showing no signs of abating and the 150.00 level likely offers resistance from here, Westpac's Richard Franulovich says in a commentary. BOJ officials' remarks continue to "err on the hawkish" side of expectations, says the head of the FX strategy group. USD/JPY edges 0.1% higher to 149.83; AUD/JPY is up 0.1% at 95.86; EUR/JPY is little changed at 157.17. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1925 ET - Acceleration in Japan's headline inflation and strength in underlying inflation in January should add to the Bank of Japan's confidence that it can maintain its monetary tightening in coming quarters, Capital Economics' Toh Au Yu says in a commentary. Inflation excluding fresh food and energy edged upward from 2.4% in December to 2.5% in January, staying well above the central bank's forecast of 2.1% for FY 2025, the assistant economist notes. Recent acceleration in underlying inflation has mostly been driven by a surge in rice inflation, the economist adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1924 ET - Air New Zealand's 1H result was better than expected by Forsyth Barr and was accompanied by a NZ$100 million share buyback, but still came with enough baggage to keep the bank at neutral for now. Air New Zealand signaled its engine maintenance challenges have worsened. It now expects to have up to 11 planes at any time grounded for maintenance in 2H, up from eight in 1H. "Air New Zealand is at least two years away from a normalized engine maintenance schedule, which leaves investors in limbo as to what is an appropriate and sustainable (as is possible for an airline) level of profitability to use for relative valuation means," analyst Andy Bowley says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1919 ET - JGBs edge lower in price terms in the morning Tokyo session, weighed by hotter-than-expected Japan CPI which may add to the case for further BOJ rate increases. Core consumer prices rose 3.2% on year in January, above economists' expectations for a 3.1% increase. Recent weakness in the domestic government bond market is likely to persist against the backdrop of revised perceptions for the BOJ's rate-hike trajectory, Mitsubishi UFJ Morgan Stanley Securities fixed income strategists say. However, declines in U.S. Treasury yields and yen appreciation may provide some support, the strategists say in a research report. The 10-year JGB yield is up 1bp at 1.450%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1848 ET - Japanese stocks may fall as concerns about borrowing costs and U.S. tariffs continue. Nikkei futures are down 0.5% at 38530 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 149.42, down from 150.14 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Japan's consumer price index for January, excluding fresh food, rose 3.2% on year, compared with a 3.1% rise expected. Investors are focusing on any developments in U.S. trade and foreign policies. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.2% to 38678.04 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1804 ET - A U.S. bankruptcy court approved Spirit Airlines' restructuring plan, which will wipe out shareholders and turn the budget airline to its bondholders. Spirit had rebuffed acquisition offers from rival budget carrier Frontier Airlines in favor of pursuing its standalone plan. Spirit said it expects to emerge from chapter 11 in the coming weeks. As part of the agreement, bondholders will swap nearly $800 million of their holdings into equity into the reorganized airline and Spirit will receive $350 million of new equity investment. "We will emerge as a stronger airline with the financial flexibility to continue providing Guests with enhanced travel experiences and greater value," CEO Ted Christie said. (alison.sider@wsj.com)

1555 ET - Two headlines have given traders reassurance about near-term supply dynamics in the Treasury market. The Fed minutes Wednesday brought an indication the central bank is considering pausing balance-sheet runoff until the debt-ceiling fight is resolved, potentially meaning the Fed's asset book may soak up more securities for longer. Then Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Bloomberg Thursday that the U.S. is still a "long way" from shifting to more long-term debt issuance in auctions, helping stem concerns about another channel of increased supply. The 10-year yield falls to 4.5%, from 4.535% Wednesday, and the 2-year yield declines to 4.26%, from 4.272% Wednesday. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1552 ET - Lean hog futures on the CME settle down 1.3% to 88.6 cents a pound, continuing the trend where lower pork cutout prices put pressure on futures. Carcass cutouts reported by the USDA yesterday fell by $3.95 per hundredweight to $95.52 per cwt. Cutouts fell another $1.28 per cwt through midday. Live cattle futures finished down 0.5% to $1.9385 a pound, ahead of the USDA's Cattle on Feed report scheduled to be released at 3 p.m. eastern time tomorrow. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1527 ET - Mexico's revised 4Q GDP numbers are expected to be largely in line with preliminary estimates, which showed economic output contracting 0.6% from 3Q in seasonally adjusted terms and rising 0.6% from the year-earlier quarter. Analysts surveyed by the Wall Street Journal expect the 0.6% drop from 3Q to remain unchanged, while output is projected to have risen 0.5% unadjusted from 4Q23. Statistics institute Inegi is scheduled to release revised numbers on Friday. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1513 ET - Some 55% more U.S. farmers declared bankruptcy in 2024 versus the previous year, although the number is still relatively low versus what was seen in the previous decade. In 2024, 216 farm bankruptcy cases were filed, says the American Farm Bureau Federation in a note, up from 139 bankruptcies in 2023. But despite the big uptick, the total number of bankruptcies is still well below the 599 bankruptcies reported in 2019 — a high point in bankruptcy cases before the disruption of the Covid-19 pandemic. But the trend is worrying, says the AFBF, especially as the USDA forecasts a decrease of crop receipts in 2025. "As the farm economy continues to be squeezed by decreased receipts and increased expenses, farm financials will be strained," says Samantha Ayoub of the AFBF. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1417 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem says that signs from interest-rate-sensitive sectors make it clear that the Fed's policy stance is still restricting the economy after a percentage point of interest-rate cuts in 2024. Slow activity in the housing sector and other industries that rise and fall with financing costs show that monetary policy remains at least a bit tight, Musalem says. "Low- and moderate-income households, and small businesses are still feeling the effects of interest rates and financing conditions," Musalem says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: West African Resources Sets 2025 Production Guidance for Burkina Faso Gold Projects
data.description:
West African Resources set a group production guidance for 2025 of 290,000 to 360,000 ounces of gold from its Sanbrado and Kiaka projects, both in Burkina Faso, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

The Kiaka project is on track to pour the first gold in the third quarter, and commercial production is expected to be declared in the second half.

The company reported Sanbrado project production guidance of 190,000 ounces to 210,000 ounces at site sustaining cost of under $1,350 per ounce and Kiaka project production guidance of 100,000 to 150,000 ounces.

It plans to conduct more than 115,000 meters of drilling across the projects in 2025, targeting depth extensions and near-surface anomalies to further increase gold resources and extend mine lives.

The firm's shares rose almost 1% in recent trading on Friday.

data.title: Singapore Shares Edge 0.1% Lower, Tracking Wall Street's Losses — Market Talk
data.description: Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index edges 0.1% lower to 3923.06, tracking Wall Street's losses overnight. Disappointing sales guidance for the coming year from bellwether U.S. retail has raised worries over the U.S. consumption outlook, Commerzbank Research analysts say in a research report. Among the worst performers on the benchmark index, Singtel falls 2.7% and Seatrium drops 2.4%.Wilmar International loses 1.9% after reporting significantly lower net profit in 2024 than the prior year. Meanwhile, Singapore Airlines rises 2.2% after posting record revenue in fiscal 3Q. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan Rate Hike Hopes Get Boost From Fresh Uptick in Inflation — Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

​TOKYO--Japan's consumer inflation grew at the fastest pace in two years in January, likely adding fuel to speculation that an interest-rate hike is coming soon.

Overall consumer prices rose 4.0% in January from a year earlier, compared with the ​3.6% growth seen in December, government data showed Friday. It was the first time since January 2023 that the overall gauge reached 4%.

Higher energy and food costs contributed to the pick-up. Energy prices rose 10.8% in January, while food prices excluding fresh food increased 5.1%.

The index excluding volatile fresh food and energy prices, a measure of underlying inflation, rose ​2.5%, ​compared with the 2.4% rise in the previous month.

​The continued rise in consumer prices likely supports expectations for an additional rate hike by the Japanese central bank. Many economists expect the bank to raise rates around the summer, but some say the bank could act sooner, after recent data pointed to the strength in Japan's economy.

The central bank raised the policy rate to 0.5% in late January from 0.25%, and BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda has pledged to keep up the increases as long as the economy and inflation develop in line with the bank's projections.

Given that consumer inflation is accelerating again due to a rise in prices of goods such as food, market expectations of an earlier interest-rate hike are likely to heighten, said Takayuki Miyajima, an economist at Sony Financial Group.

That view sent Japanese government bond yields to multi-year highs this morning. The benchmark 10-year JGB yield reached 1.455%, the highest since November 2009. The policy-sensitive two- and five-year yields rose to 0.830% and 1.095%, respectively, the highest since October 2008.

"The Bank of Japan is in a very difficult situation," Miyajima said, however. "The growth in service prices, an important indicator of underlying inflation, shows little sign of acceleration--which would make the BOJ to be somewhat cautious about rate hikes."

Friday's data showed that service prices rose 1.4% in January from a year earlier, slower than the 1.6% increase seen in December.

Some economists say wage growth could add to services inflation.

"As strong wage increases are expected to continue, the pace of service price rises is likely to remain steady," said Mizuho Securities economist Ryosuke Katagi. Still, the acceleration marked in January isn't enough for the BOJ to achieve its goal of stable 2% inflation, he added.

Japanese companies are expected to give big pay raises again at annual wage talks, the preliminary results of which are due in March.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Nine Entertainment Says Reviewing Domain Acquisition Proposal; Shares Rise 24%
data.description:
Nine Entertainment Holdings , the majority shareholder of Domain Holdings Australia , said it will assess the unsolicited, non-binding acquisition proposal from Texas-based real estate information and analytics firm CoStar Group to Domain, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

The company said Domain is of "strategic importance" to its media ecosystem and long-term growth strategy.

Shares of Nine Entertainment rose nearly 24% in recent Friday trade. Shares of Domain Holdings rose 40%, earlier hitting a three-year high.

data.title: Japan Logs Softer Decline in Manufacturing Conditions in February - S&P Global Survey
data.description:
The au Jibun Bank Japan Manufacturing PMI rose slightly to 48.9 in February from 48.7 in January, suggesting a softer decline in manufacturing conditions, according to S&P Global on Friday.

Although output and new orders declined less sharply, the sector's ongoing weakness led to job cuts for the first time since last November 2024.

Purchasing and inventory levels also saw significant reductions, said the survey.

Signs of excess capacity persisted, with firms noting a solid decline in outstanding business. Optimism for future output dropped to its lowest since June 2020.

In the services sector, the Business Activity Index inched up to 53.1 in February from 53.0 in January.

This marks the strongest business activity growth since last September. While new business growth slowed slightly, expansion plans fueled activity and sales.

Employment rose for the 17th month, maintaining workload management. Input price inflation eased a bit, resulting in the mildest output charge increase in four months.

However, confidence for the year ahead fell to its lowest since January 2021, with concerns about inflation and labor shortages affecting future prospects.
